front: Interview question that signals senior AND screens for bad on-call culture.
back: 'What's on-call like on this team? How often do people get paged, and what's the process when something breaks at 3am?' This reveals: (1) do they have SLOs and error budgets, (2) does the team respect work/life balance, (3) does leadership invest in reliability, (4) are postmortems blameless. Green flags: rotation size >=5, pages/week <2, dedicated follow-up time. Red flags: 'it's not too bad' with no numbers, solo on-call, no postmortem process.
senior_nuance: Candidates who don't ask about on-call have never been on a team with bad on-call. Seniors always ask. Follow-up: 'What's the last page that woke someone up, and did it turn into a fix?'
quote_to_say: What does on-call look like? Who gets paged and how often?

front: Question to ask that surfaces how the team treats tech debt.
back: 'How does the team decide when to invest in platform / tech debt work vs features? Can you give me a recent example where you chose one over the other?' The example is the tell — vague answers mean they don't actually do it. Green flag: named project, named tradeoff, PM involvement. Red flag: 'we do it on 20% time' with no examples, or 'we'll get to it'.
senior_nuance: Asking for a specific recent example is the pro move. Anyone can claim they value tech debt. Only healthy teams can point to last quarter's example.
quote_to_say: Give me a recent example where you picked debt work over a feature.

front: Roadmap question that reveals strategic clarity (or lack of it).
back: 'What's the team's biggest bet for the next 6 months, and what has to be true for it to work?' Forces the interviewer to state a thesis, not a todo list. Green flag: clear bet + named risks + measurable success criteria. Red flag: a list of features with no narrative, or 'we'll see what the PM prioritizes'.
senior_nuance: Ask separately of the EM, the IC interviewer, and (if you get one) a skip-level. If their answers don't match, the team lacks alignment and you'll spend energy on priority churn.
quote_to_say: What's the team's biggest bet, and what has to be true for it to work?

front: Question that uncovers what someone actually values (vs what they say in the recruiter pitch).
back: 'Why did you join, and what has surprised you — positively or negatively — since?' The surprise part is key. 'Nothing has surprised me' is suspicious. You want specific, honest answers about friction. A real person will mention one bureaucratic thing and one thing they love. Follow up on both.
senior_nuance: Ask this of everyone you talk to. Patterns emerge: if three people all say 'the interview process was long but thorough', that's signal. If three people say 'the planning process is chaotic', that's also signal.
quote_to_say: What surprised you — good or bad — after you joined?

front: Architecture question that signals senior-level thinking.
back: 'Walk me through a recent architecture decision and the options you didn't pick.' This tests whether they reason about tradeoffs or just cargo-cult. Green flag: named alternatives, explicit tradeoffs, a decision record. Red flag: 'we use X because it's what we use', no alternatives considered, or only upside, no downside. Bonus: ask how they'd redo it if starting today.
senior_nuance: Senior engineers ask about the options not taken. Juniors ask about the stack. The question 'what would you do differently' distinguishes teams that retrospect from teams that don't.
quote_to_say: Tell me about a recent architecture decision and the options you rejected.

front: Question that reveals engineering culture beyond the brochure version.
back: 'How does code review work here? Walk me through a recent PR that got a lot of discussion.' Real answers reveal: review depth, disagreement norms, blocking vs commenting style, async culture. Green flag: 2+ reviewers, design discussed before code, respectful pushback. Red flag: rubber-stamp approvals, huge PRs, 'we move fast and fix forward' (often means no review).
senior_nuance: Also ask: 'Who can push to main without review?' and 'How often does a PR from a junior change the design of a review from a senior?' You're testing whether the culture rewards being right over being senior.
quote_to_say: Walk me through a recent PR that had a lot of discussion.

front: Question that gauges team health without being awkward.
back: 'Who's left the team in the last year, and why? Who's joined?' Retention is the single most honest signal. High attrition on a senior team is a flashing red light. Also ask: 'What's the tenure of the longest person? Of the manager?' Green flag: stable manager, long tenures, departures to promotions or other great roles. Red flag: repeated departures with vague reasons, revolving-door management.
senior_nuance: You will not get a fully honest answer, but you will get a directional one. Crosscheck with LinkedIn before the interview — you can see departures even when the company won't name them.
quote_to_say: Who's left recently and where did they go?

front: Incident question that reveals operational maturity.
back: 'What was your last significant incident, and what changed as a result?' You're testing three things: (1) do they have incidents (everyone does — if they say 'none', they hide them), (2) do they do postmortems, (3) do postmortems produce durable changes. Green flag: concrete story, named fix, process change. Red flag: 'we fixed it and moved on', 'it was human error', no follow-up tracking.
senior_nuance: The phrase 'human error' as root cause is a culture red flag — mature teams treat human error as a symptom and look for the system that allowed it. Listen for that framing.
quote_to_say: What was your last significant incident and what changed after?

front: Hiring-bar question that signals you care about peer quality.
back: 'What's the hiring bar like? When was the last time you said no to a strong candidate, and why?' This reveals whether the team has a real bar or just fills seats. Green flag: concrete story about rejecting someone talented for a specific reason (collaboration gap, scope mismatch). Red flag: 'we hire anyone good', 'we don't reject many', or 'the bar is high' with no specifics.
senior_nuance: Your peers will be whoever passes this bar. If you're senior and they can't describe a rigorous no, you'll end up being the adult in every room. That gets exhausting.
quote_to_say: When did you last say no to a candidate who was technically strong?

front: Growth question to ask your future manager.
back: 'Who on your team has grown the most in the last 18 months, and what did that growth look like? What did you do to support it?' Three things revealed: (1) does the manager invest in growth, (2) is promotion possible on this team, (3) can the manager articulate someone else's trajectory specifically. Green flag: named person (no name given to you, but concrete), named skills, named opportunities the manager created. Red flag: generic 'everyone grows here'.
senior_nuance: The manager's answer to this is a mirror of what your growth will look like. If they can't describe someone else's concretely, yours won't be either.
quote_to_say: Who's grown the most on your team, and what did you do to support it?
